The Laplace Equation

April 8, 2022 . .

Aims Aims

I Most problems in fluid and solid mechanics, quantum I Most problems in fluid and solid mechanics, quantum mechanics, electromagnetism, and other areas of physics mechanics, electromagnetism, and other areas of physics can be modelled as partial differential equations (PDEs). can be modelled as partial differential equations (PDEs). I This topic introduces the method of separation of variables that solves the wave equation, heat equation, and Laplace equation.

. .  Aims What you need to know

I Most problems in fluid and solid mechanics, quantum mechanics, electromagnetism, and other areas of physics can be modelled as partial differential equations (PDEs). I Partial derivatives. I This topic introduces the method of separation of variables that solves the wave equation, heat equation, and Laplace equation. I There are many PDEs that do not have known analytical solutions, but can be solved approximately by numerical methods. MATH2089 introduces the finite difference method to solve PDEs.

. .

What you need to know What you need to know

I Partial derivatives. I Partial derivatives. I Solutions to ODEs. I Solutions to ODEs. I Fourier series expansion of a periodic function.

. .  What you will learn What you will learn

I Use separation of variables to construct solutions to a I Use separation of variables to construct solutions to a PDE. PDE. I Combine these solutions by superposition.

. .

What you will learn What you will learn

I Use separation of variables to construct solutions to a I Use separation of variables to construct solutions to a PDE. PDE. I Combine these solutions by superposition. I Combine these solutions by superposition. I Use Fourier series to fit source terms or boundary data. I Use Fourier series to fit source terms or boundary data. I Use d’Alembert’s method to solve the wave equation.

. .  What is a PDE? What is a PDE?

I A partial differential equation (PDE) is an equation I A partial differential equation (PDE) is an equation involving one or more partial derivatives of an unknown involving one or more partial derivatives of an unknown function that depends on more than one variable. These function that depends on more than one variable. These variables are often time t ∈ [0, ∞) and position ⃗x ∈ Ω for variables are often time t ∈ [0, ∞) and position ⃗x ∈ Ω for a spatial domain Ω ⊆ Rd where d ∈ {1, 2, 3}. a spatial domain Ω ⊆ Rd where d ∈ {1, 2, 3}. I The order of a PDE is the order of the highest derivative in the equation.

. .

What is a PDE? What is a PDE?

I A partial differential equation (PDE) is an equation I A partial differential equation (PDE) is an equation involving one or more partial derivatives of an unknown involving one or more partial derivatives of an unknown function that depends on more than one variable. These function that depends on more than one variable. These variables are often time t ∈ [0, ∞) and position ⃗x ∈ Ω for variables are often time t ∈ [0, ∞) and position ⃗x ∈ Ω for a spatial domain Ω ⊆ Rd where d ∈ {1, 2, 3}. a spatial domain Ω ⊆ Rd where d ∈ {1, 2, 3}. I The order of a PDE is the order of the highest derivative I The order of a PDE is the order of the highest derivative in the equation. in the equation. I A PDE is linear if it is linear in the unknown function u I A PDE is linear if it is linear in the unknown function u and its partial derivatives. and its partial derivatives. I A solution of a PDE is a function of several variables which satisfies the equation.

Superposition Superposition Theorem 5 (Principle of Superposition) Theorem 5 (Principle of Superposition) If u1 and u2 are any solutions of a linear homogeneous PDE If u1 and u2 are any solutions of a linear homogeneous PDE then for any constants a1 and a2 , the linear combination then for any constants a1 and a2 , the linear combination

u = a1 u 1 + a2 u 2 u = a1 u 1 + a2 u 2

Step 1: Apply the method of separation of variables to Step 1: Apply the method of separation of variables to obtain two ODEs; obtain two ODEs; Step 2: Determine solutions of these two ODEs that satisfy the boundary conditions;

We will see that Step 2 requires us to solve an eigenvalue We will see that Step 2 requires us to solve an eigenvalue problem for one of the ODEs. problem for one of the ODEs.

Separation of variables Eigenfunctions and eigenvalues Definition 7 Consider the homogeneous boundary-value problem, Step 1: Apply the method of separation of variables to obtain two ODEs; u′′ + λu = 0 for 0 < x < L, Step 2: Determine solutions of these two ODEs that a0 u′ + b0 u = 0 at x = 0, (16) satisfy the boundary conditions; aL u′ + bL u = 0 at x = L, Step 3: Use superposition (Theorem 5) and Fourier series (if necessary) to obtain a solution to the given where λ is a (possibly complex) parameter. equation which also satisfies the initial conditions. If u = ϕ(x) is a non-trivial (that is, not identically zero) solution, then ϕ is an eigenfunction with eigenvalue λ, for the given interval (0, L) and the given boundary conditions. We will see that Step 2 requires us to solve an eigenvalue Note: since ϕ′′ + λϕ = 0 we have problem for one of the ODEs.  2 d − ϕ = λϕ on (0, L). dx . .   Example 8 Find all eigenfunctions and eigenvalues for the problem λ = 0 Solve u′′ (x) = 0. Hence

u′′ + λu = 0 for 0 < x < L, u′′ (x) = 0 ⇒ u(x) = ax + b . u = 0 at x = 0, Next apply the boundary conditions u(0) = u(L) = 0. u = 0 at x = L. Thus

Motivated by if λ is = 0, < 0 or > 0 we obtain different u(0) = 0 = a(0) + b ⇒ b = 0 , functions as a solution to the homogeneous ODE u′′ + λu = 0 u(L) = 0 = La ⇒ a = 0 . we investigate the 3 cases separately. Thus for λ = 0 the solution is u(x) ≡ 0, i.e., the trivial solution. Note this solution is not surprising since u has to be zero at x = 0 and x = L. The only straight line (solution to u′′ (x) = 0) to be zero at x = 0 and x = L is the constant line u(x) ≡ 0.

Hence u′′ (x) − p2 u(x) = 0 ⇒ u(x) = a epx + b e−px . u′′ (x) + p2 u(x) = 0 ⇒ u(x) = a cos(px) + b sin(px) . Next apply the boundary conditions u(0) = u(L) = 0. Thus Next apply the boundary conditions u(0) = u(L) = 0. Thus u(0) = 0 = a(1) + b(1) = a + b ⇒ b = −a . u(0) = 0 = a(1) + b(0) ⇒ a = 0 , Hence, at this stage, u(L) = 0 = b sin(pL) ⇒ b ̸= 0 .  u(x) = a epx − e−px = 2a sinh(px) . Why b ̸= 0? If b = 0 then u(x) ≡ 0, i.e., trivial solution. With b ̸= 0 then We note sinh(px) is an increasing function (why?). So if a > 0 then u(x) will increase from x = 0 and if a < 0 sin(pL) = 0 ⇒ pL = nπ , n = 1, 2, 3, then u(x) will decrease from x = 0. Thus in either nπ ⇒ p= , n = 1, 2, 3, scenario u(L) ̸= 0 but the boundary condition is L u(L) = 0. A contradiction. Hence a = 0.
